statement: Despite near-100% renewable electricity, Iceland imported approximately 800,000 tonnes of fossil fuels in 2023, primarily diesel and marine fuel oil for the fishing fleet, road transport, and aviation. Fossil fuels accounted for roughly 15–20% of Iceland's total primary energy consumption. Electric vehicle adoption has been rapid — EVs and plug-in hybrids reached approximately 50% of new car registrations in 2023, up from 8% in 2018. However, decarbonising the fishing fleet and heavy transport remains challenging. Iceland's total greenhouse gas emissions were approximately 3.5 million tonnes CO₂-equivalent in 2023 (excluding LULUCF), with transport and industrial processes as the main sources.
source_name: Umhverfisstofnun — National Inventory Report 2024; Hagstofa Íslands
caveats: Emissions data is subject to methodological revisions. LULUCF (land use, land-use change, and forestry) adds significant emissions in Iceland due to degraded wetlands and erosion. The 2023 figures are preliminary estimates; the final verified inventory typically lags by 1–2 years.

related_entries.statement: Iceland generates virtually 100% of its electricity from renewable sources: approximately 70% from hydropower and 30% from geothermal energy. In 2023, total electricity generation was approximately 19.5 TWh, of which roughly 13.5 TWh came from hydropower and 6 TWh from geothermal. This makes Iceland unique among European countries — the EU average share of renewables in electricity generation was approximately 44% in 2023. Only Norway (98% hydro) comes close to Iceland's renewable share. Iceland's per capita electricity consumption is among the highest in the world at roughly 53 MWh per person per year, driven largely by aluminium smelting.

Iceland has committed under the Paris Agreement to a 55% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions by 2030 compared to 1990 levels, aligned with the EU's target through the EEA joint fulfilment arrangement. Iceland's Climate Action Plan (Aðgerðaáætlun í loftslagsmálum, 2020, updated 2024) outlines sectoral measures. However, Iceland's emissions profile is unusual: industrial process emissions from aluminium smelting are large, agriculture (sheep farming, dairy) contributes significantly, and LULUCF is a major source due to degraded wetlands covering roughly 10% of the country. Total emissions excluding LULUCF were approximately 3.5 Mt CO₂-eq in 2023, while LULUCF added roughly 8–10 Mt CO₂-eq, making Iceland a substantial net emitter relative to its population of 390,000.

citations.report_title: 390.000 hektarar af landbúnaðarlandi breytast í skóg og votlendi

claims.claim_text: Bein losun frá landbúnaði á Íslandi er um 14-15% af heildarlosun gróðurhúsalofttegunda.
Losun frá framræstu votlendi á Íslandi samsvarar um 55-60% af heildarlosun landsins.
Stærsta loftslagsáskorun Íslands tengist landnotkun og framræstu votlendi.
